target.name: cephanolide A
target.motivation: Cephalotane diterpenoids, featuring unique and complicated carbon skeletons and remarkable antitumor activities, have been gaining increasing attention. Benzenoid cephalotane-type diterpenoids are a relatively novel subtype with a unique 6/6/6/5 tetracyclic core embedded with a bridged delta-lactone, containing 5-7 contiguous stereogenic centers. Their intricate and diverse structures, as well as their potential biological activities, make them attractive targets for synthetic chemists.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: palladium-catalyzed cascade cyclization reaction to forge the 6-5-6 cis-fused tricyclic core
intramolecular Prins cyclization, followed by a cation-mediated etherification/Friedel–Crafts cyclization
intramolecular Diels–Alder cycloaddition to forge the core skeleton
tandem intramolecular Pauson-Khand reaction, 6π-electrocyclization reaction, and oxidative aromatization strategy
tandem intramolecular Pauson-Khand reaction, 6π-electrocyclization reaction, and oxidative aromatization strategy

success_criteria.free_text: Asymmetric total synthesis of cephanolides A-D (1-4) and ceforalide B (5) via late-stage modification.

key_disconnections.reasoning: to disconnect the core skeleton into two intermediates, such as aryl iodide and bridged δ-lactone

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The strategy involves a cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation and the precise construction of three C–C bonds and two chiral centers. This cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation strategy, if effective, might also prove valuable for total syntheses of a variety of natural products featuring the 6/6/6/5 tetracyclic skeleton. The key cascade C(sp2)–H and C(sp3)–H activation reaction, featuring the construction of three C–C bonds and two chiral centers accurately, allows access to the core skeleton of benzenoid cephalotane-type diterpenoids, such as 10a and 10b, from iodobenzene derivatives (11a and 11b) and alkyl bromide 12 in one step. The alkyl bromide 12 could be constructed from cyclohexenone 13 via reduction and acetalization. Ketone 13 could be prepared by Bamford-Stevens reaction and subsequent functional group interconversion (FGI) from compound 14, which could be derived from a known compound 15.

thought.gap: Need to prepare C10 unit acetal 12.
thought.decision: To prepare compound 14, alkylation of compound 15 with a 2-iodoethanol derivative was chosen, expecting a single diastereomer.

outcome.observation: Compound 14 was furnished as a single diastereomer in up to 62% yield on decagram scale.

evidence.text_span:
Our synthesis work commenced with the preparation of C10 unit acetal 12 from the known chiral compound 15, which was prepared from cyclohexenone in one step with 75% yield and 93% ee59 (Fig. 3).

The alkylation of compound 15 with a 2-iodoethanol derivative, conditions previously reported by Zhu60, furnished compound 14 as a single diastereomer in up to 62% yield on decagram scale.

thought.gap: Need to convert 14 to alcohol 16.
thought.decision: To obtain alcohol 16, a one-pot olefin formation/deprotection protocol was chosen.

outcome.observation: Exposure of 14 to a one-pot olefin formation/deprotection protocol yielded alcohol 16.

evidence.text_span: Exposure of 14 to a one-pot olefin formation61/deprotection protocol yielded alcohol 16

thought.gap: Need to convert 16 to cyclohexanone 13.
thought.decision: To obtain cyclohexanone 13, 16 was treated with CBr4 and PPh3, followed by CrO3 and 3,5-DMP.

outcome.observation: Cyclohexanone 13 was obtained, along with a small amount of compound 17. Olefin 17 can convert to cyclohexanone 13 through the same oxidation.

evidence.text_span:
which was then subsequently treated with CBr4 and PPh3,

followed by CrO3 and 3,5-DMP to obtain cyclohexanone 13, along with a small amount of compound 17. Noteworthy, olefin 17 can convert to cyclohexanone 13 through the same oxidation.

thought.gap: Need to convert 13 to allyl alcohol 18.
thought.decision: To produce allyl alcohol 18, cyclohexanone 13 was reduced by CeCl3·7H2O and NaBH4.

outcome.observation: Cyclohexanone 13 was reduced by CeCl3·7H2O and NaBH4 to produce allyl alcohol 18.

evidence.text_span: Cyclohexanone 13 was reduced by CeCl3·7H2O and NaBH4 to produce allyl alcohol 18

thought.gap: Need to convert 18 to lactone 19.
thought.decision: To form lactone 19, 18 was treated with NaH.

outcome.observation: Allyl alcohol 18 was transformed to lactone 19 in the presence of NaH.

evidence.text_span: which was transformed to lactone 19 in the presence of NaH.

thought.gap: Need to convert 19 to acetal 12, avoiding stereoselectivity issues during the cascade reaction.
thought.decision: To obtain acetal 12, lactone 19 was reduced by DIBALH to the corresponding aldehyde, which was then directly transformed into acetal 12 in the presence of chloroform and methanol to prevent the formation of the dimethyl acetal byproduct.

outcome.observation: Lactone 19 was reduced by DIBALH to produce the corresponding aldehyde, which was then directly transformed into acetal 12 in the presence of chloroform and methanol to prevent the formation of the dimethyl acetal byproduct.

evidence.text_span: Thus, lactone 19 was reduced by DIBALH to produce the corresponding aldehyde, which was then directly transformed into acetal 12 in the presence of chloroform and methanol to prevent the formation of the dimethyl acetal byproduct.

thought.background: Acetal 12 was in hand. The goal was to perform the pivotal palladium/NBE-cocatalyzed cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation to assemble the tetracyclic skeleton.
thought.gap: Need to find conditions for the cascade reaction to produce 10a.
thought.decision: An initial attempt was made with tri(2-furyl)phosphine as the ligand and Cs2CO3 as the base at 110 °C.

outcome.observation: The C(sp2)–H activation product 20a was obtained in 43% yield along with the nucleophilic substitution byproduct 21, but the cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation product (10a) was not observed.

evidence.text_span: As shown in Table 1, an initial attempt with tri(2-furyl)phospine as the ligand and Cs2CO3 as the base was conducted at 110 °C. The C(sp2)–H activation product 20a was obtained in 43% yield along with the nucleophilic substitution byproduct 21, but the cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation product was not observed (entry 1).

thought.background: The previous attempt at 110 °C yielded only the C(sp2)–H activation product 20a and byproduct 21, with no desired cascade product 10a.
thought.gap: The cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation product (10a) was not observed.
thought.decision: To obtain the desired compound 10a, the reaction temperature was raised to 120 °C.

outcome.observation: The desired compound 10a was obtained, albeit accompanied by a significant amount of compounds 20a and 21. The ratio of 10a/20a was 1/4.9.

evidence.text_span: When the reaction temperature was raised to 120 °C, the desired compound 10a was obtained, albeit accompanied by a significant amount of compounds 20a and 21 (entry 2).

thought.background: Increasing the temperature to 120 °C yielded 10a but with significant byproducts. The yield of byproduct 21 indicates that nucleophilic substitution by acetate anions is fast, rendering them unable to participate in the CMD process of crucial C(sp3)–H activation.
thought.gap: Need to inhibit nucleophilic substitution and promote the CMD process.
thought.decision: To inhibit nucleophilic substitution and promote the CMD process, KOPiv, which has a larger steric effect, was added.

outcome.observation: The yield of 10a/20a (1/4.6) decreased to 34%, while the yield of 21/22 increased to 44%. This result may indicate that the nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12 with carboxylate is much faster than the oxidative addition of acetal 12 with aryl-norbornyl-palladacycle (ANP) C.

evidence.text_span: Therefore, to inhibit nucleophilic substitution and promote the CMD process, KOPiv, which has a larger steric effect, was added. However, the yield of 10a/20a (1/4.6) decreased to 34%, while the yield of 21/22 increased to 44% (entry 4). This result may indicate that the nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12 with carboxylate is much faster than the oxidative addition of acetal 12 with aryl-norbornyl-palladacycle (ANP) C (Fig. 4).

thought.background: Adding KOPiv did not improve the yield of 10a and increased byproducts, suggesting nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12 is faster than oxidative addition with ANP C.
thought.gap: Need to mitigate the tendency of nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12.
thought.decision: To mitigate the tendency of nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12, PdCl2 was used instead of Pd(OAc)2 as a catalyst.

outcome.observation: Although the nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12 is inevitable, the yield of nucleophilic substitution product 22 is slightly reduced. The ratio of 10a/20a was 1/5.

evidence.text_span: To mitigate the tendency of nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12, PdCl2 was used instead of Pd(OAc)2 as a catalyst (entry 5). Although the nucleophilic substitution of acetal 12 is inevitable, the yield of nucleophilic substitution product 22 is slightly reduced.

thought.background: Using PdCl2 slightly reduced byproduct 22, but the overall yield of 10a was still low.
thought.gap: Need to further promote the oxidative addition of 12 with ANP C.
thought.decision: The dosage of KOPiv and delayed addition time were screened to promote the oxidative addition of 12 with ANP C. It was found that the reaction proceeded more efficiently with 1.8 equivalents KOPiv when a 60 s delay was implemented for the addition.

outcome.observation: The reaction proceeded more efficiently with 1.8 equivalents KOPiv when a 60 s delay was implemented for the addition. The ratio of 10a/20a was 4.4/1, and the yield of 22 was 73%.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, the dosage of KOPiv and delayed addition time were screened to promote the oxidative addition of 12 with ANP C (entries 6-10). It was found that the reaction proceeded more efficiently with 1.8 equivalents KOPiv when a 60 s delay was implemented for the addition (entry 7).

thought.background: Optimized KOPiv dosage and addition time improved efficiency, but the yield of 10a was still not ideal.
thought.gap: Need to further improve the yield of 10a.
thought.decision: A few other ligands were tested.

outcome.observation: No improved results were obtained with triphenylphosphine as ligand. The yield of 10a/20a was 17% (2.4/1), and the yield of 22 was 79%.

evidence.text_span: Next, a few other ligands were tested; however, no improved results were obtained (entries 11-14).

thought.background: Testing other ligands did not improve the yield of 10a.
thought.gap: Need to find better reaction conditions.
thought.decision: A few other ligands were tested, including Tris(4-methoxyphenyl)phosphine.

outcome.observation: No improved results were obtained with Tris(4-methoxyphenyl)phosphine as ligand. The yield of 10a/20a was 12% (2.2/1), and the yield of 22 was 85%.

evidence.text_span: Next, a few other ligands were tested; however, no improved results were obtained (entries 11-14).

thought.background: Testing other ligands did not improve the yield of 10a.
thought.gap: Need to find better reaction conditions.
thought.decision: A few other ligands were tested, including Tris[4-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]phosphine.

outcome.observation: No improved results were obtained with Tris[4-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl]phosphine as ligand. The yield of 10a/20a was 10% (2.2/1), and the yield of 22 was 86%.

evidence.text_span: Next, a few other ligands were tested; however, no improved results were obtained (entries 11-14).

thought.background: Testing other ligands did not improve the yield of 10a.
thought.gap: Need to find better reaction conditions.
thought.decision: A few other ligands were tested, including 1,3-Bis(diphenylphosphino)propane.

outcome.observation: No improved results were obtained with 1,3-Bis(diphenylphosphino)propane as ligand. Only byproduct 22 was obtained in 95% yield, and 10a/20a were not detected.

evidence.text_span: Next, a few other ligands were tested; however, no improved results were obtained (entries 11-14).

thought.background: Ligand screening did not improve the yield of 10a.
thought.gap: Need to find better reaction conditions.
thought.decision: Solvent screening was performed, starting with PhMe.

outcome.observation: No product was detected when PhMe was used as solvent.

evidence.text_span: In the screening of solvents (entries 15-19), the optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio (entry 18).

thought.background: PhMe as solvent resulted in no product.
thought.gap: Need to find a suitable solvent.
thought.decision: Solvent screening was performed, using PhCN.

outcome.observation: The yield of 10a/20a was <10% (1/5.6), and the yield of 22 was 33%.

evidence.text_span: In the screening of solvents (entries 15-19), the optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio (entry 18).

thought.background: PhCN as solvent resulted in low yield.
thought.gap: Need to find a suitable solvent.
thought.decision: Solvent screening was performed, using t-BuCN.

outcome.observation: The yield of 10a/20a was <10% (1.5/1), and the yield of 22 was 56%.

evidence.text_span: In the screening of solvents (entries 15-19), the optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio (entry 18).

thought.background: t-BuCN as solvent resulted in low yield.
thought.gap: Need to find a suitable solvent.
thought.decision: Solvent screening was performed, using a mixed solvent of DMF/PhMe = 1/2.

outcome.observation: The yield of 10a/20a was 28% (16/1), and the yield of 22 was 73%.

evidence.text_span: In the screening of solvents (entries 15-19), the optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio (entry 18).

thought.background: Solvent screening showed that a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio was optimal.
thought.gap: Need to optimize the reaction conditions for the cascade C(sp2) & C(sp3)–H activation to obtain 10a.
thought.decision: The optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio. The reaction was performed on a hundred-milligram scale with 1.8 equiv KOPiv and a longer delay and addition time (120 s & 50 s).

outcome.observation: On a hundred-milligram scale, compound 10a was obtained in 42% yield, and the by-product 20a was not observed under these conditions. Compound 22 was obtained in 64% yield.

evidence.text_span:
In the screening of solvents (entries 15-19), the optimal result was achieved using a mixed solvent of DMF and PhMe in a 1:1 ratio (entry 18). Notably, on a hundred-milligram scale, compound 10a was obtained in 42% yield, and the by-product 20a

was not observed under these conditions (entry 20).

thought.gap: Need to synthesize 10b.
thought.decision: Compound 10b was obtained when 11b reacted with acetal 12, with a delay in the time of adding KOPiv.

outcome.observation: Compound 10b was obtained when 11b reacted with acetal 12, with a delay in the time of adding KOPiv. The ratio of 10b/20b was 10/1, and the yield of 22 was 67%.

evidence.text_span: Compound 10b was obtained when 11b reacted with acetal 12, with a delay in the time of adding KOPiv (entry 21).

thought.gap: Need to synthesize cephanolide A (1).
thought.decision: To synthesize compound 24, compound 10a was hydrolyzed in the presence of 3 M HCl (aq).

outcome.observation: Compound 10a was hydrolyzed in the presence of 3 M HCl (aq.) resulting in compound 24. The structure of 24 was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis.

evidence.text_span: For the synthesis of cephanolide A (1), compound 10a was hydrolyzed in the presence of 3 M HCl (aq.)65 resulting in compound 24. The structure of 24 was confirmed by X-ray crystallographic analysis (see Supplementary Information).

outcome.observation: The Davis oxaziridine has been demonstrated to be efficacious in introducing a hydroxyl group at the C3 position, resulting in compound 26 with 71% yield.

thought.decision: Compound 10b was oxidized to produce lactone 29.

outcome.observation: Lactone 29 was then converted to cephanolide C (3) using the NHPI/Co(OAc)2-catalyzed oxidation.

thought.gap: Need to introduce a methyl ester group at the C15 position.
thought.decision: The selective bromination at the C15 position was attempted using TBCA/TFA.

outcome.observation: The expected compound 31a was obtained in a modest yield using TBCA/TFA, along with a small amount of nonseparable C13 brominated by-product 31b.

evidence.text_span: To efficiently introduce a methyl ester group in the late stage, the selective bromination at the C15 position was attempted. The expected compound 31a was obtained in a modest yield using TBCA/TFA67, along with a small amount of nonseparable C13 brominated by-product 31b.

thought.gap: Need to convert 31a and 31b to compounds 32a and 32b.
thought.decision: The Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation was used to convert 31a and 31b to 32a and 32b.

outcome.observation: The Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation resulted in compounds 32a and 32b.

evidence.text_span: Finally, the Pd-catalyzed methoxycarbonylation12,17 resulted in compounds 32a and 32b

thought.decision: Compounds 32a and 32b were further oxidized to cephanolide D (4) and ceforalide B (5) via NHPI/Co(OAc)2-catalyzed oxidation.

outcome.observation: Compounds 32a and 32b were further oxidized to cephanolide D (4) and ceforalide B (5) via NHPI/Co(OAc)2-catalyzed oxidation.

validation.significance: The developed cascade C(sp2)–H and C(sp3)–H activation strategy efficiently accesses the characteristic and synthetically challenging 6/6/6/5 tetracyclic skeleton of benzenoid cephalotane-type diterpenoids. This strategy enabled a short total synthesis of cephanolide B (2) and, through late-stage modification and a divergent approach, the syntheses of four additional benzenoid cephalotane-type diterpenoids (cephanolides A, C, D, and ceforalide B). The key feature is the construction of three C–C bonds (two C(sp2)–C(sp3) bonds and one C(sp3)–C(sp3) bond) and two chiral centers accurately in one step via palladium/NBE-cocatalysis. This method has potential for the preparation of various natural products.

source.title: Asymmetric total synthesis of benzenoid cephalotane-type diterpenoids through a cascade C(sp<sup>2</sup>) & C(sp<sup>3</sup>)-H activation.
source.authors: Li X, Lu Z, Liu S, Sun M, Duan S, Xie Z.
